book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
The Third Theological Oration. On the Son.

ccel.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

source.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

title: The Third Theological Oration. On the Son.

source.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Third Theological Oration.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This then is what might be said to cut short our opponents’ readiness to argue and their hastiness with its consequent insecurity in all matters, but above all in those discussions which relate to God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But since to rebuke others is a matter of no difficulty whatever, but a very easy thing, which any one who likes can do; whereas to substitute one’s own belief for theirs is the part of a pious and intelligent man; let us, relying on the Holy Ghost, Who among them is dishonoured, but among us is adored, bring forth to the light our own conceptions about the Godhead, whatever these may be, like some noble and timely birth.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Not that I have at other times been silent; for on this subject alone I am full of youthful strength and daring; but the fact is that under present circumstances I am even more bold to declare the truth, that I may not (to use the words of Scripture) by drawing back fall into the condemnation of being displeasing to God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And since every discourse is of a twofold nature, the one part establishing one’s own, and the other overthrowing one’s opponents’ position; let us first of all state our own position, and then try to controvert that of our opponents;—and both as briefly as possible, so that our arguments may be taken in at a glance (like those of the elementary treatises which they have devised to deceive simple or foolish persons), and that our thoughts may not be scattered by reason of the length of the discourse, like water which is not contained in a channel, but flows to waste over the open land.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The three most ancient opinions concerning God are Anarchia, Polyarchia, and Monarchia.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The first two are the sport of the children of Hellas, and may they continue to be so.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For Anarchy is a thing without order; and the Rule of Many is factious, and thus anarchical, and thus disorderly.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For both these tend to the same thing, namely disorder; and this to dissolution, for disorder is the first step to dissolution.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But Monarchy is that which we hold in honour.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is, however, a Monarchy that is not limited to one Person, for it is possible for Unity if at variance with itself to come into a condition of plurality; but one which is made of an equality of Nature and a Union of mind, and an identity of motion, and a convergence of its elements to unity—a thing which is impossible to the created nature—so that though numerically distinct there is no severance of Essence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Therefore Unity having from all eternity arrived by motion at Duality, found its rest in Trinity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This is what we mean by Father and Son and Holy Ghost.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Father is the Begetter and the Emitter; without passion of course, and without reference to time, and not in a corporeal manner.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Son is the Begotten, and the Holy Ghost the Emission; for I know not how this could be expressed in terms altogether excluding visible things.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For we shall not venture to speak of “an overflow of goodness,” as one of the Greek Philosophers dared to say, as if it were a bowl overflowing, and this in plain words in his Discourse on the First and Second Causes.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let us not ever look on this Generation as involuntary, like some natural overflow, hard to be retained, and by no means befitting our conception of Deity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Therefore let us confine ourselves within our limits, and speak of the Unbegotten and the Begotten and That which proceeds from the Father, as somewhere God the Word Himself saith.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: When did these come into being?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They are above all “When.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, if I am to speak with something more of boldness,—when the Father did.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And when did the Father come into being.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There never was a time when He was not.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And the same thing is true of the Son and the Holy Ghost.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Ask me again, and again I will answer you, When was the Son begotten?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: When the Father was not begotten.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And when did the Holy Ghost proceed?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: When the Son was, not proceeding but, begotten—beyond the sphere of time, and above the grasp of reason; although we cannot set forth that which is above time, if we avoid as we desire any expression which conveys the idea of time.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For such expressions as “when” and “before” and “after” and “from the beginning” are not timeless, however much we may force them; unless indeed we were to take the Æon, that interval which is coextensive with the eternal things, and is not divided or measured by any motion, or by the revolution of the sun, as time is measured.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How then are They not alike unoriginate, if They are coeternal?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Because They are from Him, though not after Him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For that which is unoriginate is eternal, but that which is eternal is not necessarily unoriginate, so long as it may be referred to the Father as its origin.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Therefore in respect of Cause They are not unoriginate; but it is evident that the Cause is not necessarily prior to its effects, for the sun is not prior to its light.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And yet They are in some sense unoriginate, in respect of time, even though you would scare simple minds with your quibbles, for the Sources of Time are not subject to time.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But how can this generation be passionless?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In that it is incorporeal.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For if corporeal generation involves passion, incorporeal generation excludes it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And I will ask of you in turn, How is He God if He is created?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For that which is created is not God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I refrain from reminding you that here too is passion if we take the creation in a bodily sense, as time, desire, imagination, thought, hope, pain, risk, failure, success, all of which and more than all find a place in the creature, as is evident to every one.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nay, I marvel that you do not venture so far as to conceive of marriages and times of pregnancy, and dangers of miscarriage, as if the Father could not have begotten at all if He had not begotten thus; or again, that you did not count up the modes of generation of birds and beasts and fishes, and bring under some one of them the Divine and Ineffable Generation, or even eliminate the Son out of your new hypothesis.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And you cannot even see this, that as His Generation according to the flesh differs from all others (for where among men do you know of a Virgin Mother?), so does He differ also in His spiritual Generation; or rather He, Whose Existence is not the same as ours, differs from us also in His Generation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who then is that Father Who had no beginning?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: One Whose very Existence had no beginning; for one whose existence had a beginning must also have begun to be a Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He did not then become a Father after He began to be, for His being had no beginning.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And He is Father in the absolute sense, for He is not also Son; just as the Son is Son in the absolute sense, because He is not also Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: These names do not belong to us in the absolute sense, because we are both, and not one more than the other; and we are of both, and not of one only; and so we are divided, and by degrees become men, and perhaps not even men, and such as we did not desire, leaving and being left, so that only the relations remain, without the underlying facts.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, the objector says, the very form of the expression “He begat” and “He was begotten,” brings in the idea of a beginning of generation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But what if you do not use this expression, but say, “He had been begotten from the beginning” so as readily to evade your far-fetched and time-loving objections?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Will you bring Scripture against us, as if we were forging something contrary to Scripture and to the truth?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Why, every one knows that in practice we very often find tenses interchanged when time is spoken of; and especially is this the custom of Holy Scripture, not only in respect of the past tense, and of the present; but even of the future, as for instance “Why did the heathen rage?” when they had not yet raged and “they shall cross over the river on foot,” where the meaning is they did cross over.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It would be a long task to reckon up all the expressions of this kind which students have noticed.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So much for this point.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What is their next objection, how full of contentiousness and impudence?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He, they say, either voluntarily begat the Son, or else involuntarily.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Next, as they think, they bind us on both sides with cords; these however are not strong, but very weak.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, they say, if it was involuntarily He was under the sway of some one, and who exercised this sway?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And how is He, over whom it is exercised, God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if voluntarily, the Son is a Son of Will; how then is He of the Father?—and they thus invent a new sort of Mother for him,—the Will,—in place of the Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There is one good point which they may allege about this argument of theirs; namely, that they desert Passion, and take refuge in Will.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For Will is not Passion.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Secondly, let us look at the strength of their argument.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And it were best to wrestle with them at first at close quarters.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: You yourself, who so recklessly assert whatever takes your fancy; were you begotten voluntarily or involuntarily by your father?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If involuntarily, then he was under some tyrant’s sway (O terrible violence!) and who was the tyrant?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: You will hardly say it was nature,—for nature is tolerant of chastity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If it was voluntarily, then by a few syllables your father is done away with, for you are shewn to be the son of Will, and not of your father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But I pass to the relation between God and the creature, and I put your own question to your own wisdom.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Did God create all things voluntarily or under compulsion?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If under compulsion, here also is the tyranny, and one who played the tyrant; if voluntarily, the creatures also are deprived of their God, and you before the rest, who invent such arguments and tricks of logic.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For a partition is set up between the Creator and the creatures in the shape of Will.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And yet I think that the Person who wills is distinct from the Act of willing; He who begets from the Act of begetting; the Speaker from the speech, or else we are all very stupid.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On the one side we have the mover, and on the other that which is, so to speak, the motion.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Thus the thing willed is not the child of will, for it does not always result therefrom; nor is that which is begotten the child of generation, nor that which is heard the child of speech, but of the Person who willed, or begat, or spoke.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But the things of God are beyond all this, for with Him perhaps the Will to beget is generation, and there is no intermediate action (if we may accept this altogether, and not rather consider generation superior to will).

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Will you then let me play a little upon this word Father, for your example encourages me to be so bold?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Father is God either willingly or unwillingly; and how will you escape from your own excessive acuteness?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If willingly, when did He begin to will?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It could not have been before He began to be, for there was nothing prior to Him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or is one part of Him Will and another the object of Will?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If so, He is divisible.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So the question arises, as the result of your argument, whether He Himself is not the Child of Will.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if unwillingly, what compelled Him to exist, and how is He God if He was compelled—and that to nothing less than to be God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How then was He begotten, says my opponent.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How was He created, if as you say, He was created?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For this is a part of the same difficulty.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Perhaps you would say, By Will and Word.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: You have not yet solved the whole difficulty; for it yet remains for you to shew how Will and Word gained the power of action.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For man was not created in this way.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How then was He begotten?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This Generation would have been no great thing, if you could have comprehended it who have no real knowledge even of your own generation, or at least who comprehend very little of it, and of that little you are ashamed to speak; and then do you think you know the whole?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: You will have to undergo much labour before you discover the laws of composition, formation, manifestation, and the bond whereby soul is united to body,—mind to soul, and reason to mind; and movement, increase, assimilation of food, sense, memory, recollection, and all the rest of the parts of which you are compounded; and which of them belongs to the soul and body together, and which to each independently of the other, and which is received from each other.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For those parts whose maturity comes later, yet received their laws at the time of conception.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Tell me what these laws are?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And do not even then venture to speculate on the Generation of God; for that would be unsafe.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For even if you knew all about your own, yet you do not by any means know about God’s.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if you do not understand your own, how can you know about God’s?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For in proportion as God is harder to trace out than man, so is the heavenly Generation harder to comprehend than your own.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if you assert that because you cannot comprehend it, therefore He cannot have been begotten, it will be time for you to strike out many existing things which you cannot comprehend; and first of all God Himself.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For you cannot say what He is, even if you are very reckless, and excessively proud of your intelligence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: First, cast away your notions of flow and divisions and sections, and your conceptions of immaterial as if it were material birth, and then you may perhaps worthily conceive of the Divine Generation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How was He begotten?—I repeat the question in indignation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Begetting of God must be honoured by silence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is a great thing for you to learn that He was begotten.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But the manner of His generation we will not admit that even Angels can conceive, much less you.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Shall I tell you how it was?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It was in a manner known to the Father Who begat, and to the Son Who was begotten.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Anything more than this is hidden by a cloud, and escapes your dim sight.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Well, but the Father begat a Son who either was or was not in existence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What utter nonsense!

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This is a question which applies to you or me, who on the one hand were in existence, as for instance Levi in the loins of Abraham; and on the other hand came into existence; and so in some sense we are partly of what existed, and partly of what was nonexistent; whereas the contrary is the case with the original matter, which was certainly created out of what was non-existent, notwithstanding that some pretend that it is unbegotten.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But in this case “to be begotten,” even from the beginning, is concurrent with “to be.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On what then will you base this captious question?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For what is older than that which is from the beginning, if we may place there the previous existence or non-existence of the Son?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In either case we destroy its claim to be the Beginning.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or perhaps you will say, if we were to ask you whether the Father was of existent or non-existent substance, that he is twofold, partly pre-existing, partly existing; or that His case is the same with that of the Son; that is, that He was created out of non-existing matter, because of your ridiculous questions and your houses of sand, which cannot stand against the merest ripple.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I do not admit either solution, and I declare that your question contains an absurdity, and not a difficulty to answer.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If however you think, in accordance with your dialectic assumptions, that one or other of these alternatives must necessarily be true in every case, let me ask you one little question: Is time in time, or is it not in time?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If it is contained in time, then in what time, and what is it but that time, and how does it contain it?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if it is not contained in time, what is that surpassing wisdom which can conceive of a time which is timeless?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Now, in regard to this expression, “I am now telling a lie,” admit one of these alternatives, either that it is true, or that it is a falsehood, without qualification (for we cannot admit that it is both).

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But this cannot be.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For necessarily he either is lying, and so is telling the truth, or else he is telling the truth, and so is lying.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What wonder is it then that, as in this case contraries are true, so in that case they should both be untrue, and so your clever puzzle prove mere foolishness?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Solve me one more riddle.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Were you present at your own generation, and are you now present to yourself, or is neither the case?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If you were and are present, who were you, and with whom are you present?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And how did your single self become thus both subject and object?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if neither of the above is the case, how did you get separated from yourself, and what is the cause of this disjoining?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, you will say, it is stupid to make a fuss about the question whether or no a single individual is present to himself; for the expression is not used of oneself but of others.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Well, you may be certain that it is even more stupid to discuss the question whether That which was begotten from the beginning existed before its generation or not.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For such a question arises only as to matter divisible by time.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But they say, The Unbegotten and the Begotten are not the same; and if this is so, neither is the Son the same as the Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is clear, without saying so, that this line of argument manifestly excludes either the Son or the Father from the Godhead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For if to be Unbegotten is the Essence of God, to be begotten is not that Essence; if the opposite is the case, the Unbegotten is excluded.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What argument can contradict this?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Choose then whichever blasphemy you prefer, my good inventor of a new theology, if indeed you are anxious at all costs to embrace a blasphemy.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In the next place, in what sense do you assert that the Unbegotten and the Begotten are not the same?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If you mean that the Uncreated and the created are not the same, I agree with you; for certainly the Unoriginate and the created are not of the same nature.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if you say that He That begat and That which is begotten are not the same, the statement is inaccurate.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For it is in fact a necessary truth that they are the same.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For the nature of the relation of Father to Child is this, that the offspring is of the same nature with the parent.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or we may argue thus again.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What do you mean by Unbegotten and Begotten, for if you mean the simple fact of being unbegotten or begotten, these are not the same; but if you mean Those to Whom these terms apply, how are They not the same?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For example, Wisdom and Unwisdom are not the same in themselves, but yet both are attributes of man, who is the same; and they mark not a difference of essence, but one external to the essence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Are immortality and innocence and immutability also the essence of God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If so God has many essences and not one; or Deity is a compound of these.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For He cannot be all these without composition, if they be essences.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They do not however assert this, for these qualities are common also to other beings.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But God’s Essence is that which belongs to God alone, and is proper to Him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But they, who consider matter and form to be unbegotten, would not allow that to be unbegotten is the property of God alone (for we must cast away even further the darkness of the Manichæans).

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But suppose that it is the property of God alone.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What of Adam?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Was he not alone the direct creature of God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yes, you will say.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Was he then the only human being?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And why, but because humanity does not consist in direct creation?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For that which is begotten is also human.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Just so neither is He Who is Unbegotten alone God, though He alone is Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But grant that He Who is Begotten is God; for He is of God, as you must allow, even though you cling to your Unbegotten.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Then how do you describe the Essence of God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Not by declaring what it is, but by rejecting what it is not.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For your word signifies that He is not begotten; it does not present to you what is the real nature or condition of that which has no generation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What then is the Essence of God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is for your infatuation to define this, since you are so anxious about His Generation too; but to us it will be a very great thing, if ever, even in the future, we learn this, when this darkness and dulness is done away for us, as He has promised Who cannot lie.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This then may be the thought and hope of those who are purifying themselves with a view to this.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Thus much we for our part will be bold to say, that if it is a great thing for the Father to be Unoriginate, it is no less a thing for the Son to have been Begotten of such a Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For not only would He share the glory of the Unoriginate, since he is of the Unoriginate, but he has the added glory of His Generation, a thing so great and august in the eyes of all those who are not altogether grovelling and material in mind.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, they say, if the Son is the Same as the Father in respect of Essence, then if the Father is unbegotten, the Son must be so likewise.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Quite so—if the Essence of God consists in being unbegotten; and so He would be a strange mixture, begottenly unbegotten.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If, however, the difference is outside the Essence, how can you be so certain in speaking of this?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Are you also your father’s father, so as in no respect to fall short of your father, since you are the same with him in essence?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is it not evident that our enquiry into the Nature of the Essence of God, if we make it, will leave Personality absolutely unaffected?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But that Unbegotten is not a synonym of God is proved thus.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If it were so, it would be necessary that since God is a relative term, Unbegotten should be so likewise; or that since Unbegotten is an absolute term, so must God be.…God of no one.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For words which are absolutely identical are similarly applied.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But the word Unbegotten is not used relatively.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For to what is it relative?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And of what things is God the God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Why, of all things.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How then can God and Unbegotten be identical terms?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And again, since Begotten and Unbegotten are contradictories, like possession and deprivation, it would follow that contradictory essences would co-exist, which is impossible.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or again, since possessions are prior to deprivations, and the latter are destructive of the former, not only must the Essence of the Son be prior to that of the Father, but it must be destroyed by the Father, on your hypothesis.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What now remains of their invincible arguments?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Perhaps the last they will take refuge in is this.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If God has never ceased to beget, the Generation is imperfect; and when will He cease?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if He has ceased, then He must have begun.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Thus again these carnal minds bring forward carnal arguments.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Whether He is eternally begotten or not, I do not yet say, until I have looked into the statement, “Before all the hills He begetteth Me,” more accurately.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But I cannot see the necessity of their conclusion.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For if, as they say, everything that is to come to an end had also a beginning, then surely that which has no end had no beginning.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What then will they decide concerning the soul, or the Angelic nature?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If it had a beginning, it will also have an end; and if it has no end, it is evident that according to them it had no beginning.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But the truth is that it had a beginning, and will never have an end.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Their assertion, then, that which will have an end had also a beginning, is untrue.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Our position, however, is, that as in the case of a horse, or an ox, or a man, the same definition applies to all the individuals of the same species, and whatever shares the definition has also a right to the Name; so in the very same way there is One Essence of God, and One Nature, and One Name; although in accordance with a distinction in our thoughts we use distinct Names and that whatever is properly called by this Name really is God; and what He is in Nature, That He is truly called—if at least we are to hold that Truth is a matter not of names but of realities.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But our opponents, as if they were afraid of leaving any stone unturned to subvert the Truth, acknowledge indeed that the Son is God when they are compelled to do so by arguments and evidences; but they only mean that He is God in an ambiguous sense, and that He only shares the Name.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And when we advance this objection against them, “What do you mean to say then?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: That the Son is not properly God, just as a picture of an animal is not properly an animal?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if not properly God, in what sense is He God at all?”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They reply, Why should not these terms be ambiguous, and in both cases be used in a proper sense?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And they will give us such instances as the land-dog and the dogfish; where the word Dog is ambiguous, and yet in both cases is properly used, for there is such a species among the ambiguously named, or any other case in which the same appellative is used for two things of different nature.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, my good friend, in this case, when you include two natures under the same name, you do not assert that either is better than the other, or that the one is prior and the other posterior, or that one is in a greater degree and the other in a lesser that which is predicated of them both, for there is no connecting link which forces this necessity upon them.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: One is not a dog more than the other, and one less so; either the dogfish more than the land-dog, or the land-dog than the dogfish.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Why should they be, or on what principle?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But the community of name is here between things of equal value, though of different nature.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But in the case of which we are speaking, you couple the Name of God with adorable Majesty, and make It surpass every essence and nature (an attribute of God alone), and then you ascribe this Name to the Father, while you deprive the Son of it, and make Him subject to the Father, and give Him only a secondary honour and worship; and even if in words you bestow on Him one which is Equal, yet in practice you cut off His Deity, and pass malignantly from a use of the same Name implying an exact equality, to one which connects things which are not equal.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And so the pictured and the living man are in your mouth an apter illustration of the relations of Deity than the dogs which I instanced.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or else you must concede to both an equal dignity of nature as well as a common name—even though you introduced these natures into your argument as different; and thus you destroy the analogy of your dogs, which you invented as an instance of inequality.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For what is the force of your instance of ambiguity, if those whom you distinguish are not equal in honour?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For it was not to prove an equality but an inequality that you took refuge in your dogs.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How could anybody be more clearly convicted of fighting both against his own arguments, and against the Deity?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if, when we admit that in respect of being the Cause the Father is greater than the Son, they should assume the premiss that He is the Cause by Nature, and then deduce the conclusion that He is greater by Nature also, it is difficult to say whether they mislead most themselves or those with whom they are arguing.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For it does not absolutely follow that all that is predicated of a class can also be predicated of all the individuals composing it; for the different particulars may belong to different individuals.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For what hinders me, if I assume the same premiss, namely, that the Father is greater by Nature, and then add this other, Yet not by nature in every respect greater nor yet Father—from concluding, Therefore the Greater is not in every respect greater, nor the Father in every respect Father?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or, if you prefer it, let us put it in this way: God is an Essence: But an Essence is not in every case God; and draw the conclusion for yourself—Therefore God is not in every case God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I think the fallacy here is the arguing from a conditioned to an unconditioned use of a term, to use the technical expression of the logicians.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For while we assign this word Greater to His Nature viewed as a Cause, they infer it of His Nature viewed in itself.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is just as if when we said that such a one was a dead man they were to infer simply that he was a Man.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How shall we pass over the following point, which is no less amazing than the rest?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Father, they say, is a name either of an essence or of an Action, thinking to bind us down on both sides.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If we say that it is a name of an essence, they will say that we agree with them that the Son is of another Essence, since there is but one Essence of God, and this, according to them, is preoccupied by the Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On the other hand, if we say that it is the name of an Action, we shall be supposed to acknowledge plainly that the Son is created and not begotten.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For where there is an Agent there must also be an Effect.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And they will say they wonder how that which is made can be identical with That which made it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I should myself have been frightened with your distinction, if it had been necessary to accept one or other of the alternatives, and not rather put both aside, and state a third and truer one, namely, that Father is not a name either of an essence or of an action, most clever sirs.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But it is the name of the Relation in which the Father stands to the Son, and the Son to the Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For as with us these names make known a genuine and intimate relation, so, in the case before us too, they denote an identity of nature between Him That is begotten and Him That begets.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But let us concede to you that Father is a name of essence, it will still bring in the idea of Son, and will not make it of a different nature, according to common ideas and the force of these names.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let it be, if it so please you, the name of an action; you will not defeat us in this way either.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Homoousion would be indeed the result of this action, or otherwise the conception of an action in this matter would be absurd.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: You see then how, even though you try to fight unfairly, we avoid your sophistries.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But now, since we have ascertained how invincible you are in your arguments and sophistries, let us look at your strength in the Oracles of God, if perchance you may choose to persuade us out of them.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For we have learnt to believe in and to teach the Deity of the Son from their great and lofty utterances.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And what utterances are these?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: These: God—The Word—He That Was In The Beginning and With The Beginning, and The Beginning.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “In the Beginning was The Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God,” and “With Thee is the Beginning,” and “He who calleth her The Beginning from generations.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Then the Son is Only-begotten: The only “begotten Son which is in the bosom of the Father, it says, He hath declared Him.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Way, the Truth, the Life, the Light.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “I am the Way, the Truth, and the Life;” and “I am the Light of the World.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Wisdom and Power, “Christ, the Wisdom of God, and the Power of God.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Effulgence, the Impress, the Image, the Seal; “Who being the Effulgence of His glory and the Impress of His Essence,” and “the Image of His Goodness,” and “Him hath God the Father sealed.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Lord, King, He That Is, The Almighty.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “The Lord rained down fire from the Lord;” and “A sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of Thy Kingdom;” and “Which is and was and is to come, the Almighty”—all which are clearly spoken of the Son, with all the other passages of the same force, none of which is an afterthought, or added later to the Son or the Spirit, any more than to the Father Himself.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For Their Perfection is not affected by additions.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There never was a time when He was without the Word, or when He was not the Father, or when He was not true, or not wise, or not powerful, or devoid of life, or of splendour, or of goodness.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But in opposition to all these, do you reckon up for me the expressions which make for your ignorant arrogance, such as “My God and your God,” or greater, or created, or made, or sanctified; Add, if you like, Servant and Obedient and Gave and Learnt, and was commanded, was sent, can do nothing of Himself, either say, or judge, or give, or will.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And further these,—His ignorance, subjection, prayer, asking, increase, being made perfect.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if you like even more humble than these; such as speak of His sleeping, hungering, being in an agony, and fearing; or perhaps you would make even His Cross and Death a matter of reproach to Him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His Resurrection and Ascension I fancy you will leave to me, for in these is found something to support our position.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: A good many other things too you might pick up, if you desire to put together that equivocal and intruded god of yours, Who to us is True God, and equal to the Father.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For every one of these points, taken separately, may very easily, if we go through them one by one, be explained to you in the most reverent sense, and the stumbling-block of the letter be cleaned away—that is, if your stumbling at it be honest, and not wilfully malicious.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: To give you the explanation in one sentence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What is lofty you are to apply to the Godhead, and to that Nature in Him which is superior to sufferings and incorporeal; but all that is lowly to the composite condition of Him who for your sakes made Himself of no reputation and was Incarnate—yes, for it is no worse thing to say, was made Man, and afterwards was also exalted.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The result will be that you will abandon these carnal and grovelling doctrines, and learn to be more sublime, and to ascend with His Godhead, and you will not remain permanently among the things of sight, but will rise up with Him into the world of thought, and come to know which passages refer to His Nature, and which to His assumption of Human Nature.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For He Whom you now treat with contempt was once above you.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He Who is now Man was once the Uncompounded.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What He was He continued to be; what He was not He took to Himself.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In the beginning He was, uncaused; for what is the Cause of God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But afterwards for a cause He was born.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And that cause was that you might be saved, who insult Him and despise His Godhead, because of this, that He took upon Him your denser nature, having converse with Flesh by means of Mind.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: While His inferior Nature, the Humanity, became God, because it was united to God, and became One Person because the Higher Nature prevailed in order that I too might be made God so far as He is made Man.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was born—but He had been begotten: He was born of a woman—but she was a Virgin.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The first is human, the second Divine.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In His Human nature He had no Father, but also in His Divine Nature no Mother.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Both these belong to Godhead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He dwelt in the womb—but He was recognized by the Prophet, himself still in the womb, leaping before the Word, for Whose sake He came into being.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was wrapped in swaddling clothes—but He took off the swathing bands of the grave by His rising again.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was laid in a manger—but He was glorified by Angels, and proclaimed by a star, and worshipped by the Magi.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Why are you offended by that which is presented to your sight, because you will not look at that which is presented to your mind?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was driven into exile into Egypt—but He drove away the Egyptian idols.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He had no form nor comeliness in the eyes of the Jews—but to David He is fairer than the children of men.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And on the Mountain He was bright as the lightning, and became more luminous than the sun, initiating us into the mystery of the future.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was baptized as Man—but He remitted sins as God—not because He needed purificatory rites Himself, but that He might sanctify the element of water.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was tempted as Man, but He conquered as God; yea, He bids us be of good cheer, for He has overcome the world.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He hungered—but He fed thousands; yea, He is the Bread that giveth life, and That is of heaven.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He thirsted—but He cried, If any man thirst, let him come unto Me and drink.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yea, He promised that fountains should flow from them that believe.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was wearied, but He is the Rest of them that are weary and heavy laden.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was heavy with sleep, but He walked lightly over the sea.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He rebuked the winds, He made Peter light as he began to sink.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He pays tribute, but it is out of a fish; yea, He is the King of those who demanded it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is called a Samaritan and a demoniac;—but He saves him that came down from Jerusalem and fell among thieves; the demons acknowledge Him, and He drives out demons and sinks in the sea legions of foul spirits, and sees the Prince of the demons falling like lightning.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is stoned, but is not taken.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He prays, but He hears prayer.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He weeps, but He causes tears to cease.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He asks where Lazarus was laid, for He was Man; but He raises Lazarus, for He was God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is sold, and very cheap, for it is only for thirty pieces of silver; but He redeems the world, and that at a great price, for the Price was His own blood.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As a sheep He is led to the slaughter, but He is the Shepherd of Israel, and now of the whole world also.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As a Lamb He is silent, yet He is the Word, and is proclaimed by the Voice of one crying in the wilderness.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is bruised and wounded, but He healeth every disease and every infirmity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is lifted up and nailed to the Tree, but by the Tree of Life He restoreth us; yea, He saveth even the Robber crucified with Him; yea, He wrapped the visible world in darkness.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is given vinegar to drink mingled with gall.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He who turned the water into wine, who is the destroyer of the bitter taste, who is Sweetness and altogether desire.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He lays down His life, but He has power to take it again; and the veil is rent, for the mysterious doors of Heaven are opened; the rocks are cleft, the dead arise.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He dies, but He gives life, and by His death destroys death.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is buried, but He rises again; He goes down into Hell, but He brings up the souls; He ascends to Heaven, and shall come again to judge the quick and the dead, and to put to the test such words as yours.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If the one give you a starting point for your error, let the others put an end to it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This, then, is our reply to those who would puzzle us; not given willingly indeed (for light talk and contradictions of words are not agreeable to the faithful, and one Adversary is enough for us), but of necessity, for the sake of our assailants (for medicines exist because of diseases), that they may be led to see that they are not all-wise nor invincible in those superfluous arguments which make void the Gospel.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For when we leave off believing, and protect ourselves by mere strength of argument, and destroy the claim which the Spirit has upon our faith by questionings, and then our argument is not strong enough for the importance of the subject (and this must necessarily be the case, since it is put in motion by an organ of so little power as is our mind), what is the result?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The weakness of the argument appears to belong to the mystery, and thus elegance of language makes void the Cross, as Paul also thought.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For faith is that which completes our argument.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But may He who proclaimeth unions and looseth those that are bound, and who putteth into our minds to solve the knots of their unnatural dogmas, if it may be, change these men and make them faithful instead of rhetoricians, Christians instead of that which they now are called.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This indeed we entreat and beg for Christ’s sake.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Be ye reconciled to God, and quench not the Spirit; or rather, may Christ be reconciled to you, and may the Spirit enlighten you, though so late.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if you are too fond of your quarrel, we at any rate will hold fast to the Trinity, and by the Trinity may we be saved, remaining pure and without offence, until the more perfect shewing forth of that which we desire, in Him, Christ our Lord, to Whom be the glory for ever.
